meta.descriptionTemplate: What changed for shipping coconut charcoal in {{dgMandatoryYear}}: IMDG Amendment 42-24 withdrew the test-out, introduced SP 978, and made declared dangerous goods mandatory.

h1: Charcoal dangerous-goods rules in {{dgMandatoryYear}}: IMDG 42-24 & SP 978
heroSubtitle: On {{dgMandatoryDate}}, IMDG Amendment 42-24 changed how coconut charcoal ships by sea. The self-heating test-out is gone; declared dangerous goods is now the only lawful route.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Ask a compliance question on WhatsApp
hero.ctaContactAria: Send an inquiry about dangerous-goods compliance

intro.p1: This page sits under the {{brand}} Logistics pillar and tracks what changed in {{dgMandatoryYear}}. For the timeless classification — what UN 1361 and Class {{unClass}} are — see the UN 1361 page; here we cover the new rules.

keyFacts.heading: What changed, at a glance
keyFacts.definition: The IMDG Amendment 42-24 changes that govern coconut charcoal from {{dgMandatoryDate}}.

whatChanged.body: IMDG Amendment 42-24 became mandatory on {{dgMandatoryDate}}. For UN 1361 it withdrew Special Provisions 925 and 223 and introduced Special Provision 978. The practical effect: the self-heating test no longer exempts charcoal from dangerous-goods rules. The cargo must be declared and carried as {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}.

sp978.body: Special Provision 978 is not just paperwork — its controls exist because contained self-heating fires have happened. Complying means the cargo is managed for that risk at source. Self-heating fire is inherent vice and is not insurable, so these prevention controls, not a policy, are the safeguard.

controls.packTempTemplate: ≤ {{sp978PackTemp}} °C on the day of packing, recorded and declared
controls.bulkValue: Containerized bulk without packaging is not allowed

sp978.cargoLinkLabel: How prevention and insurance fit together
sp978.documentsLinkLabel: The DGD data fields in the document pack

nonDg.body: No. Since {{dgMandatoryDate}} there is no compliant non-DG route for UN 1361 charcoal. Any supplier quoting non-DG freight is offering a misdeclaration — and the exposure lands on the importer: carrier blacklisting, port-state detention, and liability if the cargo self-heats. We ship declared, every time.
nonDg.shippingLinesLinkLabel: Carriers that enforce SP 978 and audits

cost.h2: What it costs, and why payload drops
cost.body: Declared dangerous goods carries surcharges — a DG premium, IMO surcharge, and DG container handling — quoted by the carrier at booking. The {{sp978Headspace}} cm headspace and stowage limits also reduce how much cargo fits, so a compliant container carries less than a brim-full one. That trade-off is the price of a cargo that arrives.

media.alt: Class {{unClass}} dangerous-goods placard on a sealed UN 1361 charcoal container
media.caption: SP 978 governs how UN 1361 charcoal is packed, declared, and stowed.

items.q: What changed in 2026 / what is IMDG Amendment 42-24?
items.a: IMDG Amendment 42-24 became mandatory on {{dgMandatoryDate}}. For UN 1361 coconut charcoal it withdrew Special Provisions 925 and 223 and introduced Special Provision 978, removing the self-heating test-out. Charcoal must now be carried as declared dangerous goods.

Special Provision 978 is the IMDG provision that now governs UN 1361 carbon. It requires weathering, a packing-temperature limit of ≤ {{sp978PackTemp}} °C recorded on the day of packing, at least {{sp978Headspace}} cm of container headspace, UN-approved sift-proof packaging, and specific declaration data — and it prohibits unpackaged containerized bulk.

No. Since {{dgMandatoryDate}} there is no compliant non-DG route. A supplier quoting non-DG freight is proposing a misdeclaration that exposes the importer to carrier blacklisting, port-state detention, and liability. We always ship declared UN 1361.

items.q: What are the weathering, packing-temperature, and headspace rules?
items.a: Charcoal is weathered after carbonization to lower its self-heating tendency; it is packed at no more than {{sp978PackTemp}} °C with the temperature recorded and declared; and at least {{sp978Headspace}} cm of headspace is left in the container. These SP 978 controls are designed to prevent self-heating in transit.

items.q: Does the new rule reduce container payload?
items.a: Yes, somewhat. The {{sp978Headspace}} cm headspace and stowage limits mean a compliant container is not packed brim-full, so net payload is lower than an unregulated load. The trade-off buys a cargo that ships legally and arrives safely.
